And for this reason the prophet sallall.aii wasallam said alkari hadith of Omar. And notice what the wording is. He says, he said to the companions, he announced to them, "Do not go back on your heels after my death, after my departure. Do not go back to kufur. Do not go back to being disbelievers. each of you striking the neck of the other meaning in battle fighting with each other. Do not go back to being disbelievers. No, of course not. You have to make and make your sujud and you have to make and you have to correct. However, this is its most prominent feature. The mere act does not take a person out of the fold of Islam. And we understand that from his words here. And so what articulated that meaning when he said with their swords and this is an important point which we will end with and that is I don't know what's in the heart of another person. I don't know could be good could be bad could be they're truthful could be they're lying through their teeth I don't know the knowledge of what is in the hearts of men is the sole domain of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. However, I do use evidence of what a person says and behaves as an evidence of what is in this person's heart. Right? So for instance, as we mentioned yesterday, if we looked at the situation of two people attacking each other with deadly weapons, all right, the sword is not used to, you know, , put someone in line. It's not used as a sportsmanlike fight or squabble. A sword is used to take life. That's its sole purpose. That's why it was designed. It was designed for everything that. similar to a handgun or a rifle, even worse. These are instruments of death, they have been designed from the ground up to take life. And so if a person uses a weapon that, even if they claim, I didn't mean to hurt him. I didn't mean to kill him. I just meant to scare him, we wouldn't accept that. Why? Because the action now speaks it articulates what is in the heart and what the intention is. We'll pick this point up inshallah tomorrow morning.

summary: This lecture analyzes a hadith narrated by Abu Bakr in which the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) states that if two Muslims fight each other with swords, both the killer and the victim will be in hellfire. The speaker explains the significance of the Prophet's specific wording, emphasizing that killing a Muslim is described as an act characteristic of disbelievers. The lecture explores the distinction between kufr al-asghar (minor disbelief referring to acts of disbelief) and kufr al-akbar (major disbelief that removes one from Islam), clarifying that fighting fellow Muslims is considered an act of disbelief while not necessarily making the perpetrator an apostate. The speaker uses examples from the Quran to illustrate how Islamic rhetoric conveys multiple layers of meaning through economical wording.
suggested_title: Understanding the Hadith on Muslims Fighting with Swords: Kufr Al-Asghar vs Kufr Al-Akbar
catchy_title: Why Killing a Muslim is Kufr
